SkyComment: Durante la observación, el cielo aparenta estar claro, sin embargo, no se pueden ver estrellas ni la luna. La fuerte presencia de luz artificial genera un efecto de brillo constante que dificulta distinguir el cielo nocturno.
LocationComment: La ubicación corresponde a un área densamente poblada. La iluminación pública y privada es abundante, lo que contribuye a la contaminación lumínica y limita la visibilidad del cielo.

SkyComment: El cielo nocturno se percibe uniforme y sin detalles visibles. No se identifican cuerpos celestes debido a la intensidad de las luces urbanas, las cuales reflejan y dispersan la luz en la atmósfera.
LocationComment: Se trata de un entorno urbano con residenciales cercanos y alumbrado público constante, lo que impide obtener una vista clara del cielo.

SkyComment: El cielo se observa mayormente despejado, pero no es posible apreciar la luna ni las estrellas. La iluminación artificial del área es muy intensa y opaca completamente la visibilidad de los cuerpos celestes. A pesar de que las condiciones climáticas parecen favorables, la contaminación lumínica impide cualquier observación astronómica.
LocationComment: La observación se realizó en un entorno urbano. Hay numerosos postes de luz, edificios iluminados y tránsito constante, lo que provoca un alto nivel de luz artificial durante la noche.

SkyComment: At the Utah site, the observation occurred well after astronomical twilight, with the Moon below the horizon. The sky was very dark and clear, with no artificial lighting visible on the horizon. The Milky Way was clearly visible and many faint stars could be observed, corresponding to a limiting magnitude of approximately 7. This represents near-pristine dark-sky conditions.
LocationComment: The dark-sky site was located near Moab, Utah, at coordinates 38.714789, −109.474825. This is a remote, sparsely populated desert area with no direct artificial lighting nearby. The nearest town is several kilometers away, and the surrounding landscape lacks streetlights or development, making it an ideal location for observing natural night-sky conditions.

SkyComment: The observation was made after astronomical twilight and after moonset, but the sky appeared bright due to artificial lighting from campus buildings and walkways. The background sky had a noticeable gray glow, and only the brightest stars were visible. The faintest stars were washed out, corresponding to a limiting magnitude of roughly 4.0, characteristic of an urban light.
LocationComment: The bright-sky site was located on the University of Colorado Boulder campus, near the Engineering Center. This location is within the city of Boulder and is surrounded by buildings, parking lots, pathways, and exterior lighting. Numerous fixtures illuminate nearby trees and walkways, producing significant localized skyglow and reducing overall visibility of faint stars.

SkyComment: Last night at 9:00 PM,I observed Orion from my school, where a few unshielded lights, a security light, and a blue-white streetlight brightened the clear sky. Using the Globe at Night charts, I estimated my NELM at 4.5, matching Bortle Class 5 suburban conditions. The experience improved my constellation confidence and awareness of lighting, and I felt proud contributing to scientific research.
The sky was clear; I don't think the Milky Way was visible. Estimated NELM was about 4.5-5.0; the view from the location was similar to Bortle Class 5, a suburban sky.
LocationComment: Near Cave Run Lake, at a public parking lookout area in Morehead, Kentucky. There are trees and hills surrounding the area, and the lake, creating darkness without any interference with streetlights or lamps. Car headlights could have altered the view of the sky, but there were none on.

SkyComment: KU – The sky was mostly cloudy again, especially toward the east and southeast, where Bootes was rising. There were a few clearer patches overhead and toward the north, so not completely overcast. I noticed a light dome again in the northwest, probably from nearby neighborhoods or streetlights. The air felt really humid, and there was a hazy look to the sky that made stars hard to see.
LocationComment: KU – Still observing from a suburban area. Same three warm-toned streetlights nearby and several porch lights on. Some trees and houses around, but the clouds were doing most of the blocking this time too.

SkyComment: KU – Most of the sky was covered in clouds, probably around 85–90%. There was a noticeable glow in the northwest and west from surrounding lights bouncing off the clouds. The air felt really humid, and there was a bit of a haze that made the sky look dull overall. I could barely make out Bootes; Arcturus was the only part I could faintly see.
LocationComment: KU – suburban spot; the usual three warm streetlights and a few porch lights around. Trees and buildings are nearby, but the clouds were the main thing blocking the sky this time.

SkyComment: KU – partly cloudy, with clouds covering about half the sky, mostly in the west and north. They weren’t super thick, just enough to block some stars in those areas. The rest of the sky was decently clear, and there wasn’t any haze that I could notice. A few of the brighter stars managed to peek through the clearer parts.
LocationComment: KU – suburban area, there’s a fair amount of lighting around. I could see three warm-colored streetlights nearby, plus a handful of porch lights from houses. There are some trees and houses around me, but they didn’t block the open sky too much.

SkyComment: AF: The sky was super clear and made it very easy to stargaze. I spotted Arcturus very quickly, and I was also able to see the fainter stars in the constellation due to the clear skies and great visibility.
LocationComment: AF: I observed from my front yard in a suburban neighborhood. There were a few lamp posts around that emitted light as well as some houses and trees that blocked my vision of the sky.

SkyComment: AF: The Sky was super clear this night and gave great visibility for stargazing. I could easily spot Arcturus, and the clear sky allowed me to see fainter stars in the constellation and make out the "Kite" shape.
LocationComment: AF: From my backyard in a suburban neighborhood. No lights around except some houses nearby. Houses also blocked some of the sky from my vision.

SkyComment: AF: The sky was partly cloudy with clouds occasionally obscuring parts of the sky. I managed to spot Arcturus, the brightest star in this constellation, but other fainter stars were not visible due to the clouds and lights around me.

With this, the sky was moderately hazy, which likely contributed to my limited visibility of the stars.

It is a pretty urban neighborhood.

SkyComment: The sky was heavily overcast, completely obscuring the visibility of any stellar objects. The dense cloud cover appeared to either absorb or reflect the artificial light emitted from the city of Boulder, resulting in a diffuse orange-yellow glow across the sky. These atmospheric conditions significantly hindered any astronomical observations.
LocationComment: The observation site was located at Panorama Point on Flagstaff Mountain, situated at a higher elevation above the University of Colorado Boulder’s Hill neighborhood. When oriented westward, the area exhibited minimal light pollution, allowing for relatively dark sky conditions. The site also offered a scenic, unobstructed landscape view overlooking the city of Boulder, Colorado.

SkyComment: The visibility of celestial objects was severely limited due to significant atmospheric haze and pervasive light pollution. Street lamps, commercial signage, and other urban lighting sources contributed to a pronounced skyglow effect, rendering it extremely difficult to locate the Leo constellation. Under these conditions, no astronomical objects were observable with the unaided eye.
LocationComment:
The observation site was a suburban parking lot located at the intersection of Baseline Road and Broadway, situated across from Kittredge Loop and adjacent to commercial establishments including Taco Bell and Wendy’s. The area is characterized by moderate to heavy commercial activity, contributing to substantial ambient light interference.

HF- The sky tonight was pretty clear, with only some clouds. I was able to clearly see the constellation Orion to the south. There were many stars that I could see in the constellation, like Betelgeuse, Alnitak, Alnilam, and Mintaka. I could also see Jupiter and Mars very well tonight. The sun was fully set when completing this observation, so barely any light was showing through the horizon.
LocationComment: HF- I recorded this information from my front yard in Kapowsin, WA. Not a very light polluted area due to Graham being the nearest town about 15 minutes away. The only other light source is a street light about 500 ft away from me. There are trees but none of them are blocking the constellation Orion.

SkyComment: HF- The sky was cloudy tonight, however, the constellation was still somewhat visible. The constellation Orion was located to the south tonight, and I could clearly see stars like Betelgeuse, Rigel, and Procyon. You could see others but they were somewhat dull. When viewing the constellation, the sun was fully set and close to no light was showing.
LocationComment: HF- I recorded this information from my front yard in Kapowsin, WA. A pretty isolated area since Graham, WA is the nearest town.  The only light source is a street light about 500 ft away from me. There are a few big trees around me, but none of them are blocking Orion.

SkyComment: Orion’s got the southern sky locked down. His belt—three bright stars lined up perfectly—stands out right away. Betelgeuse glows with this soft red, while Rigel shines cold and blue. The air’s sharp and crisp, making the stars feel like you could almost reach out and touch them. Just under the belt, the Orion Nebula hangs there, a faint misty glow, like a reminder of how big everything really is.
LocationComment: CW - I did this in the parking lot of my apartment

SkyComment: Orion stretches across the southern sky. His belt—three bright stars in a perfect line—draws your gaze first. Betelgeuse glows with a faint red shimmer, while Rigel shines a crisp, icy blue. The cold night air makes the stars feel sharper, almost closer. Just below the belt, the Orion Nebula lingers as a soft, hazy glow—a quiet reminder of the universe’s endless depths.
LocationComment: CW - I did this in the parking lot of my apartment

SkyComment: Orion stretches across the southern sky, his belt—three bright stars—perfectly lined up. Betelgeuse glows with a warm, reddish hue, while Rigel shines cold and blue. SkyComment: HF- Sky tonight had some haze and minimal clouds. A little light pollution coming from Bonney lake, but pretty minimal overall. Some of the stars and bright planets not covered by the haze were visible, like Mars and Jupiter. Even with the haze, most of Orion was visible tonight. Orion was again in the southeast, and you could see Betelgeuse, Alnitak, Alnilam, and Mintaka pretty clearly.
LocationComment: HF- Recorded Information in Wilkeson, WA. Quiet area with some light pollution from Bonney lake. One light source which is a street light about 500 ft away from me. There are some trees surrounding me but not blocking the constellation at all.

SkyComment: HF- Sky was again perfectly clear with no clouds in sight. Lots of planets and stars out tonight, like Saturn and Venus. The constellation Orion was in the southeast tonight very visible. I could once again see the stars like Betelgeuse, Alnitak, Alnilam, and Mintaka. Almost all of the constellation was visible except for the very few stars that you can’t see with a naked eye.
LocationComment: HF- Recorded information from Kapowsin, WA in my front yard. Quiet area with the nearest town being Graham 20 minutes away. LocationComment: CM - i am doing this in my front yard with a very clear view of the night sky, no trees in my field of view, its almost pitch black except for the street light around 40 feet in front of me. it was snowing about an hour before my report but it was not snowing during my report.
